interactions.summary: The host introduces the conversation, says Jiang has lectured extensively on eschatology, praises Dugin's Foundations of Geopolitics, and asks Dugin to assess Jiang's lectures and their disagreements.

Dugin says Chinese culture is normally balanced and non-linear rather than end-times oriented, then praises Jiang as an exceptional Chinese thinker who understands Jewish, Zionist, Shia, Islamic, and Russian Orthodox eschatology.

speaker_notes.note: The host is likely Sneako; Dugin begins the main answer around seg-0015.

claims.claim: Dugin claims Chinese civilization tends toward harmony and balance rather than a linear final battle between good and evil.
claims.temporal_scope: Claim made in this April 13, 2026 interview.

claims.claim: Dugin identifies Jiang as unusual among Chinese thinkers because Jiang grasps eschatological geopolitics across Zionist, Islamic, and Russian Orthodox traditions.
claims.temporal_scope: Claim made in this April 13, 2026 interview.

claims.claim: The host links eschatology to national character, saying a nation's end-times vision expresses its soul.
claims.temporal_scope: Claim made in this April 13, 2026 interview.

signature_moments.moment: Jiang is presented as the rare Chinese interpreter who can enter the radical feeling of the end.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This establishes why the interview treats Jiang as a bridge between Chinese, Russian, Islamic, and Western frames.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: The interview frame treats eschatology as an expression of the soul and historical direction of a nation.
